data.title: Boston Omaha: 'Hold' Was The Right Choice; Entry Potential Is Here
data.description:
I covered Boston Omaha (BOC) about 8 months back, citing concerns for historical negative returns and internal turmoil creating longer-term uncertainty for the company, and as a result, making it a "HOLD" rather than a "BUY", despite fundamental appeal in some areas like billboards.

Boston Omaha remains a sub-$500M company operating in theoretically attractive operations, with good results, but the market has yet to pick up on its overall appeal. The returns since the last article, found here, look like this.

The company has underperformed the market, making the "HOLD" rating correct. Provided that we have an operationally positive result, this could make the company more interesting here. But BOC is, due to its size and exposure, a company that I do not focus on too frequently - updating during earnings updates and quarterlies. Also, as I have covered in my previous article, due to its tricky operating model resulting in negative earnings year after year (not expected to change in 2025 or 2026), evaluating it with typical or traditional methods is tricky. It means that in most cases, as I invest, there are more attractive companies around.

The company's earnings being negative has explanations (net other expenses, looking at financials, which are related to things like investments and operating cost increases, as well as some D&A), and while it might not offer a "fair picture" of the company, it is what investors tend to focus on.

In this article, I will be covering recent results and why they are tricky, even when considering the resilience of billboards and the like.

Boston Omaha - Even the resilience argument does not carry the company here

Some core issues for BOC, that I see, include a tapering-off rate of revenue growth. Current results and estimates do not guide for impressive forward growth, nor is it about to repeat the trip from 2017 to 2024, which saw the company increase its revenues 9-10x.

The positive is, on the other hand, that EBITDA and earnings are potentially expected to increase in a year or more, though due to aforementioned negatives, the net income is still expected to be in the negative.

Boston Omaha is not exactly a frequently covered business on Seeking Alpha. The last article was in April, almost 8 months ago. That means 2 full quarters have gone by without anyone touching upon the company's investment appeal, or lack thereof.

The positive thesis for Boston Omaha is typically centered around its broadband business as a growth driver, addressing needs in an area that is otherwise not well-covered by peers. It is also typically characterized as a longer-term value play, with a market cap that does not match its earnings potential going forward.

For the time being, color me unconvinced by the positives for the company.

I could write the company's stock price reaction off as unfavorable results in 2Q and 3Q, with continued net losses (even now, as of the latest report), as well as profitability issues, and the question of what exactly would make the company grow. The company has repeatedly missed analyst estimates for not only revenues, but also for earnings. The company's weakness when a multiple-sector company underperforms is starting to show here, with Sky Harbour being the most obvious example. Last period, the company saw an unrealized loss of $10M for the investment. The company (Sky Harbour) further has a book value of less than $100M, but a supposed value of over $170M, which creates potential valuation risk in case of debt/interest (which the company has), and because BOC is exposed to this, this is a risk for BOC as well.

The case for the company would be very different if its core operations—billboards, broadband, AM, and insurance—were profitable enough to generate positive earnings and offset the negatives in these investments. It is not, not as of the latest results either. I consider the market reaction to the company's negatives entirely fair, maybe even a little too much of a drop given everything.

BOC does not have a dividend. You, as the investor, get nothing except capital appreciation - and when the stock drops, you literally get nothing (and lose money). The company lacks a dividend; the company has a sub-$390M market capitalization as of the time of writing this article.

Arguments for investing in this company are, as I see it, few and far between. The thesis early in 2025E was cash flow increases and stability from core, with a focus on long-term value creation. The argument has often been "the market is not realizing its value".

Some of the positive arguments that can be made about the company are, as I see them, not to be disputed. Do I think the company's businesses, meaning advertising, broadband, and so on, will still exist a decade from now? I would say yes, they will. Do I think the company is in danger of immediate bankruptcy? My answer would be a firm "no". The company's balance sheet, despite everything, is relatively solid here. Less than 16% long-term debt to capital makes the business interesting from that perspective.

Less interesting is the 7-year 2-year based forecast accuracy of missing targets negatively 100% of the time, or the current forecasts, which put AEPS in negative territory not only for 2025E, but for 2026E as well. 3Q25, which was released yesterday, confirms this. Negative $0.08/share for EPS (GAAP), a significant miss once again, and a revenue miss as well.

At some future point, I have no doubt that the company will be attractive and a "BUY". But I do not, at this time, believe it to be the time to invest in Boston Omaha.

There is a reason I gave the company a PT of $11/share. We are still at $12+ today. So Boston Omaha has further to drop before I would be willing to "get in" here.

Let me run you through my assumptions again.

Valuation for Boston Omaha - Unattractive at this valuation

Because of the negative earnings and net results, we cannot use P/E for this company. Well, we could, but we would reach a negative 97x P/E for the company as of right now, and despite an expected 40% AEPS growth rate, the expectation is negative.

So let us avoid that.

What do we do with a company with negative earnings? We need to focus on metrics that are asset-based or forward-looking. We can use revenue multiples, book value, and other methods. Advanced analysts who work in the space may also try the VC valuation method (for venture capital), where an analyst would work backward from a future "exit" value - but that would be less relevant here.

Unfortunately, things do not get much easier with sales or revenue multiples. What I can clearly say is that since 2020, the company's revenue multiples and sales multiples are a lot less than they were - it is down from over 5-8x, which would be too high, to about 3-3.6x in terms of both. But whether this is in any way appealing will, of course, depend on what the company does with its revenues, with its cash.

Boston Omaha is no longer followed by analysts from S&P Global, as far as I can see. The last-updated price target is 3 months old, and details a low-end price target of $14/share as well as a higher-end of $22/share, implying an upside close to 100% if you believe that one.

The best method that is sort of my "fallback" here is what the company is worth per share in terms of book value. At this particular time, the total book value on a per-share basis, meaning the total balance sheet assets per the company's 31.45M shares, comes to $16.8/share. But before you jump for joy, remember that the company has done write-downs of its assets and taken hits for its investments.

Still, I view the book value as the relevant multiple here, properly discounted. I previously discounted book value at 35%, down to about $11.2, making it $11/share to account for all the investment risk in Sky Harbour, as I see the other segments (advertising, broadband) as cash-generative businesses with a good future, especially advertising and broadband.

There is some truth to the idea that the market has yet to "wake up" to what Boston Omaha offers. The problem is, I think, that it will be some time before it might be justified for the market to actually wake up.

As it stands, even holding your money in plain cash with zero interest would have been a better investment than buying Boston Omaha. That is not a favorable comparison. To those who argue that Sky Harbour could be a "gem," yes, it could. It could also be nothing. Based on the company's almost unfailing negative earnings, a forecast failure of 100%, and an LT/debt to capital of almost 75%, it is not my idea of a "safe investment" currently.

You might ask what you could buy instead, while waiting to see what happens with Boston Omaha?

I would go for Realty Income (O) as an option. 5.71% on cash monthly, A-rated and with proven stability and a current upside of double digits on a 20-year average AFFO basis. It is one of the few REITs I still hold actively and invest in.

But Boston Omaha? That is a company that I would currently avoid.

Risks & Considerations

Risks for Boston Omaha are, to me, very clear. The company is a smaller-cap business without any sort of yield, and with negative net earnings, impacted by continual D&A and investment losses. While the company's core business lines generate impressive safety and stability, for the past few years, the other businesses' losses and write-downs have impacted the valuation, despite the market at the time of writing being almost at a "high" or peak valuation. This makes arguing for an investment here very tricky.

The reason is that if the company has been reporting losses, and is continuing to expect to report losses (as it seems to be at this time), then why would the valuation turn around in the hypothetical situation that the market crashes? I view it as far more likely that the company will drop alongside the market. This means that even if you are bullish on Boston Omaha, you should consider investing when the company gets cheaper.

And that, dear readers, is what makes this company such a tricky play and a "HOLD" with a PT of $11 to me. I even considered lowering the target.

Thesis

  • Boston Omaha has many investors convinced of its fundamental value found in attractive subsidiaries and holdings, which are performing very well.
  • However, I believe this is a good example of where the market simply will not value a stock as "you want" due to management complications and lack of clarity. The company has been on a new journey since mid-2024, but I fail to see any near-term catalysts that could change the share price to anywhere positive, where I could make a good return, buffeted by good safety. This forecast, which I gave in early 2025, has turned out to be exactly the right way to handle this.
  • I, therefore, say that BOC is a "HOLD" and give the company a share price of $11/share, about 30% below the current lowest analyst share price out there (from S&P Global). This was the target in my last article, and it remains my target now - the recent results give me no reason to bump this.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically is not enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $11/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of November 2025.

data.title: DLocal Q3: The Market's Concerns Are Overstated As TPV Surges
data.description:
DLocal Limited (DLO) reported impressive Q3 results on the 12th of November. The payment processor showed very impressive financials as payment volume on DLocal’s platform continues to surge, going unappreciated by the market. The stock is down nearly -9% at the time of writing, seeming to reflect selling pressure that is unrelated to DLocal’s earnings strength.

I maintained a Buy rating in my previous May article on the stock, titled “DLocal Q1: Payment Volume Surge Offsets Take Rate Concern”. The stock has since returned 26%, outperforming the S&P 500’s 15% gain.

DLocal’s Q3 Growth Was Highly Impressive

Contrary to the market’s negative reaction, I believe that DLocal’s Q3 financials were overall very good. The company focuses on emerging markets, causing some market volatility, but the Q3 report still mostly reflects DLocal’s strength.

Despite DLocal’s growing scale, the company’s growth rate even accelerated. Total payment volume “TPV” growth reached 59%, four percentage points above the year-to-date rate. Revenues reached $282.5 million, up at a nearly similar 52% year-on-year rate. Gross profit reached $103.2 million, up by 32% year-on-year – growth metrics remained fantastic all around. Most of the growth was achieved with existing merchants’ growth, as the TPV retention rate stood very strong at 157%, and new merchants provided a very slight further boost to growth. By vertical, TPV growth came in strong within ecommerce at 87%, and remittances showed even faster 226% growth. Most verticals showed good double-digit percentage growth, continuing DLocal’s growth story.

Revenues beat Wall Street’s consensus by a significant $20.5 million margin; DLocal’s growth remains far above the market’s assumptions.

As is most often the case with DLocal, the performance again varied across markets. Brazil showed especially strong growth, followed by strength in Colombia, Bolivia, and Nigeria. Mexico, Argentina, and Egypt, on the other hand, showed significant weakness on a sequential basis. The weakness reflects tariffs and a shifting payment mix in Mexico, lower interest rate spreads and increased processing costs in Argentina, and a share-of-wallet loss in Egypt with one large merchant. Macroeconomic turbulence continues to cause some volatility in DLocal’s outlook due to tariffs, shifting regimes in Brazil, and currency volatility in Argentina, Egypt, and Bolivia. Overall, DLocal’s strong growth in certain markets still continues to carry growth past weakness in others; macroeconomic turbulence hasn’t been enough to disrupt DLocal’s increasingly diversified business.

The Take Rate Causes Concern, Again

The market’s main concern seems to be DLocal’s take rate trajectory; competitors’ discounting seems to still be causing pressure on DLocal’s pricing. The net take rate declined by 8 basis points year-on-year to 0.99%, again causing quite a large gap in difference between TPV and gross profit growth. An increasing share of payouts, weakness with the large merchant in Egypt, currency spread weakness, and several other factors continue to weigh on DLocal’s take rate, according to the company. Ultimately, it seems that competition is causing DLocal’s weaker pricing power with merchants. The take rate previously stabilized on a sequential basis in Q2 but now fell again. The performance gives space for previous concerns around the take rate. DLocal’s take rate has now halved since 2022.

Regardless, I believe that DLocal’s growth story is intact—take rate pressure hasn’t been nearly enough to offset DLocal’s impressive momentum in TPV as gross profit continues to surge. While the rate declined again, DLocal's take rate has still shown signs of stabilizing during 2025; pricing pressure seems to be slowing down. On top, DLocal’s cost discipline continues to make growth very accretive to earnings. Adjusted EBITDA reached $71.7 million, up by 37% year-on-year. The quarter’s $0.21 adjusted EPS beat Wall Street’s consensus by four cents.

DLocal’s 2025 guidance remains on track. TPV growth was previously guided at 40-50%, but the company now expects to exceed the guidance range. Yet, the gross profit and adjusted EBITDA guidances have stayed intact at 27.5-37.5% and 40-50% growth, respectively. Incremental TPV has come at the price of the take rate.

DLO Stock Remains Undervalued

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for DLocal. I have kept growth assumptions quite similar, reflecting accelerating TPV growth but a declining take rate. I now estimate the 2024-2034 revenue CAGR at 15.4% instead of the previous 14.7%. I again assume 3% perpetual growth afterwards.

DLocal’s impressive cost discipline has caused margins to expand faster than I previously estimated. I again estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 24%, but that DLocal achieves the margin level by 2027 already. As previously, the company should convert after-tax earnings very well into free cash flow.

The estimates put DLocal’s fair value at $19.2, 42% above the stock price at the time of writing. The stock still has incredibly good upside, enabled by the market’s overblown take rate concern.

The fair value estimate is up from $16.5 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 11.60%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume DLocal to leverage a noteworthy amount of debt. For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.11% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 9.59% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent average July estimate for Mexico, Brazil, and Nigeria. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.75. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 11.60%.

Takeaway

DLocal reported very good Q3 results as payment volume growth accelerated in many markets. Take rate pressure, seeming to stem from competitive pressure, hasn’t caused DLocal’s earnings growth to halt despite the market’s concerns around the factor. As DLO stock remains undervalued, I remain with a Buy rating.

data.title: EPS: An Earnings-Weighted ETF That Can't Beat The S&P 500
data.description:
Investing in large-cap companies today can be a source of worries because of several factors, such as the increasing concentration of the market, growth of average multiples, and increasing share of growth companies in the S&P 500. In this environment, it can be natural to seek alternatives to the S&P 500, and today I will look at one of them with the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Fund (EPS), an ETF applying an earnings-weighting methodology to invest in large caps, which is designed to be an alternative to the S&P 500 as it also focuses on the 500 largest U.S. companies.

I will rate the fund a hold. I don’t believe that it could be considered as a replacement for the S&P 500. The fund presents some interesting features, such as lower valuations, a slightly higher dividend, but the risk-adjusted performance is just not as good, and I don’t see a compelling point that justifies paying a higher expense ratio against other ETFs such as the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO). The fund is not bad, but I don’t think that there is an investment case here, hence the hold rating.

Two Ways To Invest In The 500 Largest Companies

While EPS and the S&P 500 are aimed at the same companies—the 500 largest U.S. equities by market capitalization—the way they construct their portfolios is different. I will use VOO as a reference for the S&P 500, and the well known index is using a market capitalization weighting methodology. This is the market’s benchmark, and it is often said that it is close to impossible to beat it in a consistent way. While this might be true, I do believe that constructing a better portfolio than simply following investment flows is possible, and this is something that EPS is trying to do. Instead of focusing on market capitalizations, which are introducing speculation as a factor, EPS is taking a different route.

The fund is tracking the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Index, and weighing companies depending on their earnings. The idea is to look away from speculation trends and only focus on a fundamental measure of profitability. This is what is considered a “smart beta” methodology: “rules-based investment strategies that aim to outperform a capitalization-weighted benchmark.” On average, this kind of strategy fails at beating the market by 1%, but this doesn’t mean that there is not one out there that works. The goal of this analysis will be to see if the approach of EPS beats the market, and I can already tell you that it doesn’t.

To select companies, the investment universe of the fund is the WisdomTree U.S. Total Market Index, the largest 500 companies are selected, and each year, the fund rebalances its composition with the earnings of the year: "earnings-weighted in December of each year to reflect the proportionate share of the aggregate earnings each component company has generated."

A Close Race, but a Clear Winner

The first natural thing to look at is the performance.

As you can see from the above, apart from very short-term investment horizons, EPS is strictly failing at beating the market. The total returns are close, but EPS lags behind. Now, this is too preliminary to say that EPS is not as good; we need to turn to risk-adjusted returns. As the weighting methodology is different, maybe the fund’s positioning, rooted in fundamentals, will prove to be more resilient than the one of VOO (it actually is not, as you will see).

Let’s look at the volatility, with the standard deviation. For EPS, we have 12.41% (3 years), 15.40% (5 years), and 15.27% (10 years) against 12.87% (3 years), 15.74% (5 years), and 15.14% (10 years) for VOO. EPS appears to be less volatile in the short to medium term, but more volatile in the long term. It is hard to draw a conclusion from this, but it is a start to explain the differences in risk-adjusted returns, measured with Sharpe ratios. For EPS, Sharpe ratios are 1.21 (3 years), 0.90 (5 years), and 0.77 (10 years) against 1.28 (3 years), 0.91 (5 years), and 0.84 (10 years) for VOO. The Sharpe ratio is taking both total returns and volatility into account, and here it is clear that EPS is a consistent risk-adjusted underperformer, putting the fund in the smart beta underperformers category.

When we look at risk-adjusted returns, the story is similar. The Sharpe ratio, a measure of return per unit of risk, is consistently slightly higher for VOO across 3, 5, and 10-year periods.

A small element in favor of EPS is the slightly better capital preservation potential. Over the last 5 years, the fund’s maximum drawdown was -22.80% against -23.90% for VOO. This is not a big enough difference to make an investment case for EPS, but it still is something.

Now, the problem of EPS is that the underperformance is coming at a premium. The fund’s expense ratio is 0.08%, not so expensive, but almost three times more expensive than a traditional S&P 500 investment in VOO with an expense ratio of 0.03%.

Portfolio and Sector Tilts

As I mentioned earlier, both funds are invested in the same companies: the 500 largest. However, the weighting methodology is introducing some key differences. If we start with concentration, the top 10 of EPS accounts for 41.43% of the portfolio, a higher degree of concentration than the 38.74% of VOO, but its top 3 is more diversified: it accounts for 20.03% against 21.28% for VOO.

As you can see from the top 10 holdings table below, there is a significant overlap in key holdings: the largest companies are often the most profitable ones (but not always).

As you can see, EPS is less invested in tech companies than VOO, and as most of the market gains of the current market are driven by tech innovation, the performance of EPS is mechanically lagging. These companies are mostly growth companies, and their performance is driven by future growth expectations, not necessarily by the current earnings.

What could be interesting is that the fund is providing investors with a larger exposure to financial companies. Lately, this has been a winning bet, as large-cap financials have benefited from important margins due to higher interest rates, an opposite trend from smaller companies that suffered because of the lower quality of their balance sheets. Right now, however, the outlook for financial companies is uncertain, mainly driven by interest rate dynamics, AI transformations, potential economic slowdowns, and regulations. Lower interest rates will compress margins but also stimulate the demand for credit; it is hard to actually predict how financial equities are going to behave. For now, interest rates are still at a high level, but the fact that the portfolio of EPS is rebalanced annually worries me, as I don’t think that the positioning of the fund will adapt fast enough to the changes of the industry. Investing in financial companies with a backward-looking earnings approach is not a good thing for me; it should be done about interest rate projections.

Other Considerations

In terms of income, as it focuses on earnings, EPS is doing slightly better than the S&P 500. The fund is distributing a dividend yield of 1.27%, backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 1.30% against 1.12% for VOO. This is a small income premium, not enough to shift the case.

EPS is not a new fund. Its inception date is 02/23/2007, and by then, the fund had only accumulated AUM of $1.3 Bn, less than 1000 times less than VOO. This goes to say that the market is not giving it much attention, and I agree with the investor’s sentiment. Over the last year, the fund saw net inflows of +110 Mn, reflecting a small continued appetite.

The fund’s average daily trading volume is $3.8 Mn (0.30% of the fund), which is small and results in a median bid/ask spread of 0.06%. This spread, because of the low daily trading volume of the fund, could widen disproportionately during events of market stress, making the fund’s liquidity at risk. This is also a key concern when in front of that you have the market leader VOO and its median bid/ask spread of 0.00%.

EPS comes with increased liquidity risk, concentration risk, underperformance risk, and costs, only to provide investors with better income and lower valuations. Not convincing.

Conclusion

A hold rating for EPS appears natural to me. The fund is not terrible in terms of performance but lacks a buy argument with regard to other alternatives in the market. The “smart beta” idea can be relevant, and I gave buy and strong buy ratings to such ETFs in the past, but I don’t think that the design of EPS will ever work to earn a long-time buy rating. EPS will never be a replacement for the S&P 500.

data.title: Niagen Bioscience: A Breakout Quarter That Now Faces Its Biggest Market Shift In Years
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I give Niagen Bioscience (NAGE) a hold rating because, although the company had a record quarter, it still faces an even more competitive landscape in the NAD+ market given the FDA's recent reversal on NMN. Tru Niagen sales still remain robust, and revenue growth is supported further by the company's expanding e-commerce, B2B distribution, and their rapidly growing footprint in the clinic space. The company also had record cash flow and has launched a share repurchase program to end their ten-year era of stock dilution. Nevertheless, it's still unclear as to whether the company's momentum in terms of revenue was durable or simply represents NMN's two-year absence from the marketplace. Either way, until post-NMN demand is clear and the impact of share repurchases becomes visible in the company's numbers, I believe the company merits a hold rating.

Earnings Recap

Niagen reported another record quarter due to three primary factors: strong double-digit growth, higher margins, and greater cash flow.

Revenue

Let's start with revenue. The company is up 33% YoY to almost $34 million—beating estimates by $2.9 million. This growth in net sales was driven by the company's strong momentum in their Tru Niagen product line, which itself is up an impressive 44% YoY to $26 million.

All this was supported by e-commerce sales of $19 million, up 29% YoY, and a 109% YoY surge in distribution from Watson's and new B2B partners totaling $7 million. Niagen's ingredient business remained steady at $6.9 million in comparison to last year's Q3 reported at $6.7 million, while pharmaceutical sales were down 44% YoY, reflecting limited demand in the company's early expansion of their distribution of Niagen Plus IVs and injectable therapies to over 1,000 clinics nationwide.

Other Metrics

Gross margins are up by one percentage point to 64.5% from last year's 63.5%, which was, in the words of CFO Ozan Pamir, "Driven by still some of the leftover inventory, the lower cost inventory we had, and also improved product mix." Sales and marketing expenses decreased to 25.8% of sales from last year's 27.5%, even though the company continued to invest in both e-commerce and clinical outreach. Meanwhile, operating income climbed to $4.2 million from $1.6 million in Q3 2024, and adjusted EBITDA more than doubled to $6.4 million up from last year's $3.5 million. The company's GAAP EPS also beat consensus by $0.04, and, of course, this is reflecting the company's higher revenue and improved profitability in comparison to last year.

Cash, Share Repurchases, and Guidance

The company ended the quarter with $64.3 million in cash and no debt, up from $60.5 million in Q2, after generating $12.8 million in cash from operations through the first nine months of 2025—already surpassing their FY 2024 $12.1 million generated in cash from operations. And, following the quarter, management launched a share repurchase program under which the company will repurchase up to $10 million of outstanding common stock over the span of about 24 months, and they also reaffirmed their full-year guidance for 25-30% YoY revenue growth.

My Analysis of Niagen's Earnings

To me, Niagen's Q3 results confirm everything I said in my previous article on the company: that this is a healthy, cash-generating machine; it's now generating revenue from places that didn't previously exist before, such as new B2B partnerships, a growing clinical network via its Niagen Plus IV and injectable therapies, and the development of a telehealth channel that will no doubt be a catalyst for FY 2026. CEO Robert Fried elaborated on this channel in the latest earnings call:

We are developing our own telehealth capability where one could go to truniagen.com or niagenplus.com and get a prescription from a physician online, much like the classic telehealth companies, and it would be delivered to their home via a pharmacy. But that -- we don't expect that functionality to be available for maybe two quarters probably, middle of next year.

And I don't want to understate the distribution business that grew 109% YoY with $7 million in revenue because it indicates that the company is having success leveraging its sales with third-party channels, which acts as a force multiplier now that the company is moving away from having to depend solely on its own website and marketing to now being able to rely on its distributors, clinics, and practitioners to drive their growth.

Meanwhile, their Tru Niagen product line that is up a whopping 44% YoY indicates that the product's demand is accelerating, which is extremely important given that the NAD+ market is both a crowded and a competitive category. And it's this NAD+ market that I want to touch on briefly before getting into the rest of my earnings analysis:

The NAD Market: The Reappearance of NMN

I explained in my last article that the goal of Tru Niagen as a dietary supplement is to boost NAD+ levels to what they were when NAD+ levels were at their peak in our early 20s, with NAD+ being essential for our cells to create the energy that powers our bodies and is at the forefront of aging and longevity science. There are two paths to increasing NAD+: one is through nicotinamide riboside [NR], which is what Tru Niagen is a patented form of, and then there's nicotinamide mononucleotide [NMN]. I don't want to get into the science behind their differences because it would be beyond the scope of this article.

But all the investor really needs to know is that back in November 2022, the FDA reclassified NMN as a drug ingredient and not as a dietary supplement, causing sellers such as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) to ban the products from its marketplace as of March 13, 2023. This, in effect, gave Niagen Bioscience greater market share and was a boon to their revenue; I would know because I used to buy both Tru Niagen and NMN through Amazon, but after the ban I just continued to use Tru Niagen exclusively.

However, the FDA has just reversed its stance, and NMN is now lawful for use in dietary supplements. This reversal took place in late September, right around the end for the earnings period for Q3. Thus, the financial result of this reversal for the company won't appear until their next earnings report, which happens to be their annual report.

Nevertheless, the company did make several comments on this in their earnings call, with one of the major comments being that they believe the FDA's decision will face strong opposition, and, thus, the company expects further challenges to it. But when asked later by analyst Susan Anderson about how the NMN reversal affected the purchasing behavior by consumers of their own products, Robert Fried responded with the following statement:

It's only been 5 or 6 weeks, and we haven't noticed anything yet. ... there's a very good chance that the FDA will reverse this reversal again ... And as you know, we've tested many of the existing NMN products on the market and very few of them actually met label claims. Some of them had actually no NMN at all. We think NMN has in its purest form, an ability to elevate NAD, not as well as Niagen, obviously, but it still does it. It's still an effective way to elevate NAD. But at this point in time, we're not seeing any meaningful impact from the change in that rule.

Thus, though the company refrained from commenting as to how the decision will affect revenue, they don't seem fazed by the FDA's reversal at all. However, I think there are three ways to look at it:

The first is the bullish position, which is that the two-year NMN product ban has allowed the company to capture a greater market share, thus giving them a larger loyal customer base that won't bother needing to switch to NMN since they are already satisfied with Tru Niagen; this would explain much of the company's revenue boon as of late, and I, personally, believe this to be the case because I happen to be one of those consumers satisfied enough with Tru Niagen and feel no need to switch to NMN.

The second is the base position, which is the same as the above, except that revenue may not be able to sustain its current momentum given that consumers now have more options for increasing their NAD+ levels, thus warranting a more cautious approach to the company.

And the third is the bearish position, which is the belief that the company's revenue boon was directly caused by NMN's 2023 ban from the marketplace and, therefore, the FDA's reversal will stagnate or possibly even reverse the company's current revenue momentum given that consumers now have more options for increasing NAD+.

As stated above, we won't know for certain until we see the Q4 revenue numbers for October, November, and December, but it does warrant investors to be cautious given the uncertainty surrounding the FDA's decision.

Further Earnings Analysis

So now, beyond the company's revenue momentum and the latest NMN reversal, there are three other aspects of Q3 that stand out to me as being the most relevant for investors:

First, the company's cost structure is becoming one of its competitive assets, and we can see that with the company's sales & marketing that has improved to being 25.8% of revenue even though the company is expanding across e-commerce, distribution partnerships, and growing its network of clinics. This is on top of operating income tripling and adjusted EBITDA doubling, which indicates this main idea: profitability is rising faster than revenue as the business scales.

Second, though pharmaceutical-grade sales fell 44% YoY, making the segment not very meaningful as of today, it can be a catalyst that could change the company's profile going forward into 2026-2028. Much of Niagen Plus IV and injection therapies are suppressed by high clinic pricing. Here is Robert Fried in his own words:

One headwind that we've noticed so far is ... the price to consumers of getting these IVs at this point is quite high. ... We think that until those prices come down to more manageable levels, the volume is not going to reach its potential. So right now, it's not a huge business for us, this pharmaceutical ingredient business that's catering to Niagen Plus. And we don't think it's going to really take off in a significant way until those two things happen. Those two things being, number one, the injection market, particularly the telehealth market embraces it. And number two, the overall pricing at the clinics and physicians' offices comes down fairly dramatically.

So considering, in the latest earnings call, that management's estimate of there being as many as 10,000 potential clinic and physician locations where Niagen Plus or injection products can be offered to patients, in addition to the company's telehealth channel for patients to get their Niagen Plus prescriptions through an online doctor, all this indicates that the long-term opportunity for this segment is much larger than what the current revenue numbers show.

Third, I personally believe that, after looking at all these latest earnings figures, the strong cash flow is the greatest case to be made for an investment into the company. As I stated above for the earnings results, the company has generated more cash from operations in three quarters than it did for FY 2024—and that's on top of the company remaining debt-free. This is vital for a biotech company that needs strong cash flow for research and clinical expansion without needing to dilute the company further. And though the company recently announced its $10 million share repurchase program, this amount may not be large enough to move EPS, but it is a signal that management sees the stock as undervalued relative to the cash it currently generates.

Investor Takeaway

Q3 was impressive, there's no doubt about it: There was accelerating demand for Tru Niagen, rapidly expanding distribution, and more cash generation in three quarters than in any other prior year—all factors that give management more strategic flexibility. There are also multiple levers, such as e-commerce, B2B partners, a growing footprint in clinics nationwide, and an up-and-coming telehealth platform that could be a future catalyst for the company in the NAD+ space.

But while the company delivered a record quarter, I still think it has yet to be determined as to whether or not the company's achievements and momentum can be maintained in a post-NMN marketplace. And there is still the issue of stock dilution:

When I had published my last article on Niagen Bioscience back in September, I assigned the company a hold rating for one major reason: because although the company and their numbers looked great, Niagen's historic addiction to stock dilution had resulted in their shares outstanding increasing by 47.53% since 2016, with another 14.2% potential dilution from outstanding equity awards. These shares outstanding have now increased, though slightly, from their total of 79.75 million in Q2 to their now roughly 79.81 million in Q3, as seen in the YCharts below.

Because there were so many shares, I had to value the company using a DCF model and calculated their intrinsic value to be just $4.04; there were just too many slices of the company's pie to merit a higher valuation.

Now that the company has launched their share repurchase program, given their record cash generated from operations, it will hopefully mark the end of their ten-year stock dilution era. Though buying back $10 million of common stock may not make much of a difference to the company's intrinsic value, it's a start that investors should take note of.

In sum, the next two quarters will determine whether Niagen Bioscience's growth was a durable inflection point or a temporary lift from a competitive gap given by the FDA. And until the post-NMN marketplace trends are clear and the share repurchases become visible in the numbers, I believe a hold rating to be the most appropriate.

data.title: Church & Dwight: Hammered Down As Expected, Upgrading To Hold
data.description:
Intro

In summer, on 22 July 2025, I threw a look at Church & Dwight (CHD) stock for the first time. Back then, I rated the stock a "sell," expecting shares to decline. This was a correct call, as CHD stock continued to decline from its highs made earlier this year, being down about 14% since my analysis.

After this decline, CHD stock is now down even over a five-year period, not counting dividends.

Investors and analysts might wonder, when this correction will be over. After having gone through the recent Q3 results, I upgrade my rating from "sell" to "hold". CHD is performing better than many peers who are coping with weak pricing and volume developments. Not so for Church & Dwight.

First signs of a stabilization

Church & Dwight reported robust Q3 2025 results, exceeding expectations amid a volatile consumer environment that's causing headaches for many consumer staples companies. But some, like CHD, are competing better. Both, the stock price (smaller declines) and especially the operating results, do reflect that. CHD is building positive momentum, as I will show now.

Revenue reached $1.59 billion, up 5% year-over-year, surpassing consensus estimates of $1.53 billion. Organic sales grew 3.4%, driven by 2.5% volume gains and 0.9% from pricing and mix, fueled by strong performances in core brands like Arm & Hammer laundry and the recently acquired Touchland hand sanitizer. The latter posted double-digit growth. This is important – Church & Dwight showed both, volume AND pricing strength.

As a result, CHD upped its previously too cautious guidance.

Adjusted EPS rose 2.5% to $0.81, supported by gross margin expansion. Gross margins are still expected to decline modestly, but less than previously guided, thanks to strong progress on efficiency and cost control. Productivity initiatives delivered 170 basis points of savings, offsetting 200 basis points of inflation and tariff pressures. CHD is not complaining, but acting.

Operating cash flow surged 19.6% to $435.5 million, with year-to-date share repurchases totaling $600 million, reflecting confidence in capital allocation, and making use of the lower share price. Just back the envelope, $600 million at the current market cap of $20 billion is 3% of shares outstanding. That's an acceptable pace, noticeably benefiting per-share results.

EPS over the last twelve months reached one their highest levels over the last five years. With strong operating momentum and buybacks in full swing, it should be only a matter of time until a new high in EPS can be reported. The guidance for the year sees $3.49.

Sooner or later, this will be reflected in the stock price.

Other achievements included market share gains in key categories (as said, many consumer staples companies are struggling) and successful integration of acquisitions. Increased marketing investments were absorbed without eroding profitability, while positive retailer line reviews signal sustained shelf strength, as was said during the conference call.

Cash flow is ticking up, having reached $1.14 billion over the last twelve months. Management, after the strong third quarter, upped the outlook for cash flow, now expected to be $1.2 billion for the full year.

Challenges remain, though. Notably, the planned discontinuation of low-margin businesses, expected to reduce Q4 sales by $30 million (200 bps impact) as inventory clears. This should be only short-term noise, strengthening CHD’s profile and margins over time. Macro volatility and input cost pressures persist, though largely mitigated through successful operational execution.

For the full year 2025, CHD raised adjusted EPS guidance to $3.49 (up $0.02) and cash flow from operations to ~$1.2 billion, while holding organic sales growth at 1% due to, or, seen positively, despite the Q4 headwind. Q4 sales are projected to grow ~3.5%, supported by innovation and acquisitions.

Looking ahead, management expressed optimism around portfolio resilience and bolt-on M&A opportunities, with an Investor Day in January to outline long-term growth strategies. Overall, CHD demonstrated defensive strength and operational excellence in a challenging landscape.

That's why I reward this positive development with an upgrade from "sell" to "hold".

CHD stock, though not dirt-cheap, is now showing a much better setup. After the share price decline, the valuation has improved. With an enterprise value of ~22 billion ($20 billion market cap + ~$1.9 billion net debt), vs. operating cash flow of $1.2 billion (~$1.1 billion FCF), the FCF multiple has reached 20x, or an expected FCF yield of 5%. There is room for improvement – that's why I don't give a "buy" rating – but together with the convincing operating performance and solid single-digit growth (supported by buybacks), high single-digit returns are possible with CHD stock over time.

But for a better rating, I'd like to see the valuation drop more.

Risks to My Thesis

As CHD stock has shown a strong performance recently, beating estimates and its own previous guidance, it is possible defensively oriented investors discover this consumer staples jewel, sending the stock price higher (my rating would be too defensive in this case).

The opposite could happen, too. An unexpected consumer backdrop and/or a second wave of input cost pressure could compress margins again, and with it, the stock price.

Although not my highest-probability scenario, CHD could be acquired by a bigger company, as $20 billion in equity value is not a hurdle high enough. This would be great for shareholders, but invalidate my current rating.

Conclusion

Church & Dwight is showing a strong operational performance and positive momentum. I like the fact that CHD is able to push both, volumes and prices, proving its strength. In consequence, I am upgrading my previous "sell" rating to a solid "hold".

data.title: Domino's: Finally Time To Bite (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In today's stock market, timing and stock selection are key. In my view, you won't earn much in 2026 by simply holding the S&P 500, but there are pockets of opportunity in several unloved industries.

In the food and restaurant space, Domino's (DPZ) is quickly emerging as a winner in a bloodbath for restaurant stocks in the Q3 earnings season. Despite the fact that Domino's itself posted very strong Q3 results, backlash against the industry after a string of poor earnings from the likes of Chipotle (CMG) and CAVA (CAVA) has spilled over to Domino's as well, and now the stock is down for the year, creating a potential buying opportunity.

I last wrote a neutral article on Domino's in August, when the stock was trading higher at $450 per share, upgrading the stock from a prior sell rating. Today, with the share of Domino's even lower despite acceleration in systemwide sales growth (a notable achievement in a quarter where most restaurants have reported resoundingly weaker growth rates), I'm upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

For investors who are newer to Domino's, here are the core reasons to be long on this stock:

  • Winning U.S. market share in a difficult climate. Many U.S. restaurant chains are reporting vast slowdowns in comp sales growth, while Domino's is reporting the opposite: same-store sales acceleration. The company is achieving this success via a continued focus on delivery and off-premises revenue, as well as promotions that are appealing to value-conscious customers (and despite these promotions, average ticket prices still rose 1.3% in Q3).
  • Asset light. There are more than 21k Domino's locations across the world, and Domino's itself only owns 260 stores in the U.S., or just over 1% of its store footprint. The company's expansion, driven primarily by franchisees, is not limited by its own balance sheet.
  • Aggressive expansion plans in China and India. The company is targeting to open 300 stores a year in China and 200 a year in India. Domino’s franchise-first strategy positions the company for high-margin recurring revenue streams without direct risk.
  • Delivery network that is not reliant on partners. Where many restaurants are crumbling now is in their reliance on the likes of Uber Eats (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH), whose fees are eating into their margins. Domino's recently rolled out its DoorDash partnership, which it expects to be an incremental acceleration to U.S. comps in Q4, adding on top of an existing agreement with Uber Eats. That said, the fact that the company has its own delivery services gives it a keen competitive advantage.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 Recap

Let's now go through Domino's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Domino's revenue grew 6% y/y to $1.15 billion, which beat Wall Street's expectations of $1.14 billion (+5% y/y) by a one-point margin. Meanwhile, revenue growth also accelerated one point versus 5% y/y growth in Q2. As a refresher, the majority of Domino's revenue comes from its supply chain (selling raw ingredients to its franchisee restaurants), and supply chain revenue grew 7% y/y to $697 million, reflecting 61% of the company total.

Of course, all eyes are on the company's same-store sales growth (which reflects the sales growth in existing store locations, excluding the contribution of newly opened and newly closed stores). What stands out to me is the fact that U.S. same-store sales accelerated to 5.2% y/y growth, up 180bps sequentially versus 3.4% growth in Q2.

International slowed slightly, but largely held pace at 1.7% same-store sales growth versus 2.4% growth in Q2. What makes Domino's success stand out is the comparison versus other large restaurant chain peers.

Chipotle, whose poor results have cut the stock in nearly half relative to where it began in 2025, reported just 0.3% comparable sales growth (a slight improvement versus a -4.0% decline in Q2, but well below the 1.0% growth that the Street was expecting):

As a more direct competitor in the fast-food space, McDonald's (MCD) turned in 2.4% comps growth in the U.S. and 3.6% overall comp sales growth, with the U.S. weakening 10bps from 2.5% in Q2, and overall company weakening 20bps from 3.8%.

It's quite evident from Domino's acceleration to mid-single-digit comps while peers are showcasing flat to low single-digit growth that the company is gaining share. Like McDonald's, Domino's is focusing on winning customers through a spotlight on promotions and value items, and per management's commentary on the Q3 earnings call, this strategy is winning market share for Domino's:

It was a great Q3 for our U.S. business. We grew in all areas key to our success. Our carryout business was positive, our delivery business was positive, and our order count growth was positive. All of this resulted in meaningful market share growth [...]

Let's start with our Best Deal Ever promotion, which was a meaningful driver of our strong U.S. results in Q3. In my opinion, Best Deal Ever is well the best deal in restaurants. The price point screens renowned value and the taste drives our most delicious food perceptions. After all, consumers are building and eating their dream pizzas. In a world where prices have gone up and discounts never seem to be on the items you truly want, Domino's gives customers their favorite pizzas at our best price. Best Deal Ever also highlights the operational excellence our system has achieved."

Management notes as well that the success of recent promotions plus menu changes have improved profitability for franchisees, which is important for franchisees to be encouraged to continue growing their store footprints. The company notes that its relaunch of Bread Bites has been a big success for deal-oriented customers ($6.99 for 32 bread bites via a Mix and Match deal), while allowing franchisees to remove Bread Twists which were operationally more difficult to produce.

On the profitability front, Domino's operating income grew 12% y/y to $223.2 million, reflecting a 19.5% margin that improved 110bps y/y. The company benefited from a 70bps jump in gross margins in its supply chain business. The company also showed tremendous OPEX discipline, keeping general and administrative spending roughly flat y/y and reducing it by 50bps as a percentage of revenue.

Domino's free cash flow of $495.6 million in the first three quarters of FY25 also jumped at a rapid 32% y/y clip.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

Despite the fact that Domino's recent performance is outstripping its peers, Domino's now trades at a discount to most large-cap peers in the broader restaurant sector. As shown below, the stock's 17x EBITDA and 23x P/E multiples sit well below other fast-food and fast-casual peers:

Of course, there are also risks that we should keep in mind here. Domino's still maintains a substantial debt load at a 4.5x leverage ratio. We note as well that few food categories are as crowded as pizza, which will make it difficult for Domino's to distinguish itself outside of price promotions.

That said, I'm enthusiastic about Domino's prospects as its same-store sales growth races ahead of peers despite the stock's declining multiples. Take advantage of this dislocation as a buying opportunity.

data.title: The Honest Company: Reducing Complexity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The share price of The Honest Company (HNST) fell 25% to $2.49 following its 3Q 2025 results announcement. It was not just the huge revenue miss that caused investors to dump the stock but rather the announcement of yet another transformation plan (dubbed Transformation 2.0). Investors have endured a lot with HNST since its IPO and were looking forward to finally being on a growth trajectory (growing sales and expanding adjusted EBITDA margin).

I will elaborate more on it below, but the gist of Transformation 2.0 is focusing on higher margin products and channels. This means reducing product categories and sales channels, which will immediately lead to a drop in sales (the categories that company plans to drop accounted for 21% of 9M 2025 sales). However, the remaining product categories are both fast growing and high margin. Add to it that a simpler product portfolio should also lower SG&A costs. Thus, though it may seem that this action is bad for growth (reducing sales) it should be good for EBITDA (profitability). Accordingly, by having a much leaner product portfolio, HNST will be able to achieve more with less.

In my view, HNST is a high risk, high reward investment. The share price has corrected substantially and is now pricing a lot of decelerations in sales. However, if Transformation 2.0 achieves its objectives, the stock will rally on growth in profitability. The road ahead will be very bumpy. I expect 4Q 2025 to be another weak quarter, weighed down by the high restructuring costs the company guided for. It is likely that some of the restructuring costs will spill into 1Q 2026. As a result, 2Q 2026 is the earliest quarter that should begin to show meaningful improvement in adjusted EBITDA due to Transformation 2.0.

Transformation 2.0: Breaking the Numbers

On June 2, 2025, Curtiss Bruce became the new CFO of HNST, and only a few months later came the Transformation 2.0 announcement. That is unlikely to be a coincidence, as Transformation 2.0 appears to be more numbers driven than an operational turnaround. Transformation 2.0 is a simple pivot towards higher margin categories where HNST has demonstrated strength. This entails simplifying the product offering to focus on just wipes, personal care and diapers and exiting the apparel business. The second part of the plan is shutting down costly pathways to the consumers, which includes ending sales through Honest.com and exiting Canada. This plan signals that management will focus on categories and channels where the economics make sense.

In the 3Q 2025 earnings presentation the company introduced the term “organic revenue”, which points at the revenue from the categories and channels that it intends to retain. Based on 9M 2025 figures, organic revenue stands at $224 million, representing 79% of total revenue. Thus, the immediate impact of Transformation 2.0 is a 21% reduction in sales. The question is whether this contraction in sales could generate higher profits.

HNST generated a gross profit of $110 million in 9M 2025 that implies a gross profit margin (GPM) of 38.8%. If we assume that the discontinued categories and channels generate a GPM of 28.8% (10 percentage points lower than the company average), it implies that the organic categories have a GPM of 41.4%. Based on this assumption, it would mean that HNST would be losing only 15% of its gross profit as opposed to 21% of sales.

Building further on this assumption, a GPM of 41.4% should translate to an adjusted EBITDA margin of 9% for the organic business (as opposed to the group total adjusted EBITDA margin of 6.4% in 9M 2025). This means that the 9M 2025 adjusted EBITDA has scope to rerate upwards by around $2 million (or 11%), from $18.1 million to $20.2 million based on my calculations. This essentially highlights how HNST will likely be able to do more with less. The cherry on top being that those are the higher growth categories.

Figure 1 Estimating Adjusted EBITDA for the Organic Business

A Layered Guidance

During the 3Q 2025 earnings call, management provided guidance that tells two stories. First, the updated full year sales guidance was brought down significantly to a growth of -3% to 0% compared to growth of 4% to 6% from the previously issued guidance. This was a painful revision for investors. However, given that the company is ending several categories and channels in 4Q 2025, it means there must be a silver lining somewhere. That’s where the guidance for organic revenue comes into focus, as it still points towards a 4% to 6% growth. This part of the guidance means that management is still seeing momentum in sales in the categories that matter despite facing a weaker consumer environment. The lower guidance for adjusted EBITDA is not surprising if a company is winding down some parts of its business. It likely means that some segments will still incur costs as revenue drops for some time until the business is fully right sized.

Moreover, management announced during the call that due to Transformation 2.0 the company will incur a one-time cost of $25 million to $35 million. They did not elaborate on whether this will be taken in 4Q 2025 alone or if it will spillover into 1Q 2026. This will be a tough pill to swallow, but a necessary step in the company’s effort to reduce complexity.

Figure 2 Summary of FY 2025 Guidance

Balance Sheet Looks Capable of Absorbing Stress

The next two quarters will see HNST incur large losses arising from restructuring costs related to Transformation 2.0. It is a good thing that HNST has a solid balance sheet, whereby it has zero debt and $71 million in cash. This provides the buffer needed to absorb such a shock without needing to raise any capital, even at the higher end of restructuring costs forecast (and assuming all are cash costs). Thus, the cash-rich balance sheet gives management enough flexibility to execute the transformation plan.

Key Risks

This is the second transformation plan for HNST. There are no guarantees that this plan will work or that it will lead to better profitability. Moreover, it is impossible to rule out a third transformation plan by the end of next year. So, this is a very risky bet on a company that doesn't have a great track record for success. Another risk worth noting is the weak macro conditions facing consumers, which was heavily emphasized by management in the 3Q 2025 earnings call. Given that HNST products sell at more premium pricing, the company could face slower sales than anticipated. Consumers could downshift to save money, and this leaves the diapers category the most exposed.

Valuation

HNST trades at an EV-to-Sales (TTM) ratio of 0.57x based on a share price of $2.52, which is well below the consumer staples sector median of 1.49x. However, TTM sales level for HNST needs to be adjusted to match the organic revenue. Thus, by reducing the TTM sales by 20% (equivalent to the 9M2025 ratio of organic revenue to total revenue), we get an adjusted EV-to-Sales (TTM) ratio of 0.71x. This is still well below median for the consumer staples sector. This shows that the valuation has a lot of scope to rerate upwards if Transformation 2.0 proves to be a success.

Figure 3 Valuation Metrics

Final Thoughts

HNST is starting to think small in order to grow. HNST was behaving like a large consumer goods company by juggling multiple products, many of which were not getting traction in the market. Focusing on its strength in wipes will allow it to grow within that niche. Moreover, relying on Amazon for e-commerce makes strategic sense when the two main products sold are wipes and diapers. A simplified product line coupled with a focused go to market strategy should drive growth in profitability. This could be an interesting phase for HNST. I am assigning a Buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Marten Transport: One Of The Top Candidates In Trucking
data.description:
Marten Transport (MRTN) is a trucking company specialized in temperature-controlled transportation. Its share price has fallen heavily together with most of the stocks in the sector. There are many reasons behind the decline, such as a dauntingly long freight recession, low freight rates, higher costs, supply chain disruptions, and finally tariffs. As a result, many smaller companies have gone out of business, and trucking companies are reluctant to invest in new equipment.

Marten appears to be one of the safest companies to invest in for the potential turnaround in the freight market. So far Marten’s business has remained profitable, it has large insider ownership, and most importantly, it has a debt-free balance sheet. Marten also pays a modest dividend, and the dividend yield has climbed to an unusually high level.

A trucking company with a focus

Marten has four different businesses: Truckload, Dedicated, Brokerage and MRTN de Mexico. Truckload is its largest business, bringing in 48% of the revenue, followed by Dedicated with 30% share of revenue. Approximately 60% of Truckload and Dedicated revenue is from temperature sensitive deliveries. At the end of 2024, Marten had 3000 company and contractor tractors, 5400 trailers, and 3800 employees. Marten has a market cap and enterprise value of approximately $800 and $750 million.

In the midst of numerous headwinds, Marten’s revenues have decreased for ten quarters in a row. The year 2024 ended with a 15% revenue decline and a 63% drop in operating income. In the first nine months of 2025, Marten’s revenue clocked in at $673 million compared to $733 in the comparison period, a decline of 8%. In the third quarter the decline was approximately 7%. Operating income dropped 31% to $18.3 million in the first nine months.

A top candidate in the industry

Hopes of freight market recovery have been pushed forward many times over. The recent industry reports still give a mixed picture. According to ACT Research, freight market has moved from sharp contraction to extended correction. Volumes are still soft, but spot rates show signs of a slight increase, and driver availability is in balance. The trucking capacity is adjusting to the current market situation, low volumes and prices, in two main ways. Existing trucking companies are exiting the industry as costs have risen much faster than freight rates. Secondly, sales of new tractors are below the required rate to maintain current fleets.

Marten has responded to the difficult market situation in multiple ways. It has cut capital expenditures heavily. Typically it invests $120-150 million, but this year Marten estimates spending $64 million. Marten maintains a young fleet of tractors. In 2024 the average age was only 1.9 years, and it typically replaces them 4 years after purchase. Unfortunately, the prices of tractors and refrigerated trailers have increased significantly, 17% and 30% compared to 2021, which showcases the level of cost inflation trucking companies have faced.

Focusing on temperature-controlled transportation exposes Marten to more stable end markets such as food and beverage. Perhaps surprisingly, the focus has not protected it from a larger revenue fall compared to many of its peers, such as Covenant Logistics (CVLG), Knight-Swift (KNX) and Werner Enterprises (WERN), which have posted better performing toplines. In response to lower volumes, Marten has increased its customer base by adding 70-100 new customers per year for the past three years, which could increase its earnings power if volumes and rates recover. Still, around half of Marten’s revenue comes from the top 10 customers, posing a risk from potential customer loss.

Recently, Marten appointed Randolph Marten as a CEO to replace retiring Timothy Kohl, who served as president of the company for 17 years. The replacement is also a Marten veteran and a former CEO between 2005 and 2021. From this perspective, the company should be in safe hands, but it is worrisome if the company cannot raise new suitable candidates for the position. Randolph Marten owns 21.8% of the company shares, aligning his work rather well with the shareholders.

In September, Marten sold its intermodal assets, including 1200 refrigerated containers, to Hub Group (HUBG) for $52 million. Intermodal business represented only 4% of Marten’s revenue. The sale strengthens Marten’s balance sheet and increases Marten’s focus on truck transportation. On the other hand, it narrows Marten’s offering in refrigerated transportation. In the first nine months, the revenue of Intermodal decreased 26%, so the sale should help Marten to report better comparison figures in the coming quarters.

Marten has a strong balance sheet to endure the difficult times. It has no long-term debt. Thanks to the sale, Marten’s cash position increased to $50 million in Q3 compared to $17 million at the year's end. Its strong financial position will help the company to have a full focus on business development and operational efficiency.

Attractively valued, but big upside is not yet visible

As Marten’s shares are still in a clear downtrend, the earnings estimates have also been heading lower recently. In the past months, earnings estimates have been revised down from $0.3 to $0.2 per share, to a level last seen in 2009-2010. So far it appears that estimates have dropped even faster than the share price. There are three analysts following Marten with an average target price of $11. Also, the target price has been revised significantly lower in the past few months.

In the past ten years, Marten’s earnings per share have averaged $0.77. When excluding the boom years of 2021 and 2022, when earnings reached $1-1.35 per share, the average still stands at $0.67. Analysts estimate Marten to reach EPS of $0.4 in 2027, which indicates expectations are rather low for the coming years.

Key questions are what will be the level of earnings in a more normal market environment, what is an acceptable valuation for Marten, and when a market turnaround happens. For the first two, we can try to make estimates based on history, while the last one likely remains the biggest question mark.

Over the very long term, 20 years, Marten’s net income margin has averaged approximately 5.5%. Over the past decade, the average margin has averaged 7%. If we assume that Marten’s revenue grows to one billion, higher than in 2024 but significantly lower than 2022 and 2023, and a net income margin of 6%, the earnings per share would reach $0.74. The P/E multiple averaged 17.4x during 2019-2022, while net income margin averaged 7.8%.

If we apply a P/E multiple of 15x, we could see a share price of $11.1, which is not meaningfully higher than the share price today. An investor needs to expect that Marten’s shares will be valued higher and/or the profitability will inflect higher, which might be difficult to achieve due to the cost inflation.

On the surface, Marten appears to be valued in line with its peer group. Considering the qualitative factors, especially the balance sheet, and profitability, Marten fares well. Most of its peers are valued higher, posting lower profitability and carrying more debt. Over the past decade, Marten’s EV/EBITDA has averaged 6.7x and P/B 2x. The discount to average multiples is well explained by the current business trajectory and profitability.
